data.title: Franklin U.S. Focused Growth SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equities delivered solid gains in 2025’s third quarter (3Q25), buoyed in part by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). Central bank actions—notably the US Federal Reserve’s interestrate cut and the potential for further monetary easing—supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. Global information technology (IT) stocks were volatile but ultimately topped all 10 other major equity sectors, supported by robust earnings and forward guidance from many IT and tech-adjacent firms—especially those tied to AI, cloud, data infrastructure, semiconductors and software.
  • Detractors: Stock selection in communication services, consumer discretionary, industrials and health care.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in IT, especially in the software and IT services industries; stock selection in the financials sector; and a supportive stock selection and underweighting in the hotels, restaurants and leisure industry.
  • Outlook: While we believe the excitement around the progress in both agentic AI and Generative AI (GenAI) as well as data-driven experimentation is merited as a widening array of industries test AI’s novel business applications, other parts of the economy are also exciting—including aerospace and defense, robotics, genomics and green energy.

Performance Review

  • The U.S. Focused Growth strategy (pure gross of fees and net of fees) had mixed 3Q25 results and lagged the Russell 1000 Growth Index based on security selection and, to a lesser extent, detrimental allocation choices.
  • In the communication services sector, support from Roblox’s (RBLX)(overweight) impressive rally was more than offset by a much larger investment in key detractor Meta Platforms (META) as its share price fell slightly.
  • Relative returns were further curbed by selloffs or subpar gains in several off-benchmark or overweight holdings, including Amazon.com (AMZN) and MercadoLibre (MELI) (e-commerce), Axon Enterprise (AXON) (aerospace and defense industry), Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) (health care equipment and supplies), ServiceNow (software), electronic trading platform operator Tradeweb Markets (TW), and Costco Wholesale (COST) (consumer staples).
  • Turning to the key contributors, the strategy was invested in three IT-related industries (out of six in total), all of which contributed to relative performance. In the software industry, heavily overweighted stakes in AppLovin (APP) and Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) surpassed the benchmark return, including an outsized gain of more than 100% for AppLovin. An underweighting in Microsoft (MSFT) also helped, as it rose less than half as much as the index. In IT services, off-index Shopify (SHOP) more than doubled the benchmark return. Most semiconductor and semiconductor equipment stocks also boosted relative returns, led by off-index Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and overweight Broadcom (AVGO).
  • NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom and other semiconductor holdings (covering almost 24% of total net assets at September-end) continue to benefit from high demand in AI and data center applications. Rapid AI-related growth boosted their revenues, particularly in areas such as graphics processing units and high-bandwidth memory. We’re seeing continued expansion in the foundry and memory markets, underpinned by AI and cloud infrastructure needs, as well as demand for device miniaturization. Semis and the broader IT sector also received US government support through tax incentives and a campaign to boost the country’s AI initiatives and tech manufacturing. The United States eased export restrictions on certain AI chips to China, reviving investor confidence in sustained demand and supply continuity. Broader policy support, including America’s AI Action Plan (announced in July), reinforced perceptions of the semiconductor industry’s strategic importance.
  • The other key individual contributors were situated outside the IT sector and posted quarterly gains that surpassed the index by a wide margin, including Robinhood Markets (HOOD) (financials).
  • Aside from our active equity holdings, returns versus the benchmark were further enhanced by the portfolio’s lack of exposure to underperforming real estate, energy, utilities and materials sector companies.

Outlook

  • We are enthusiastic about the potential and current productivity gains from AI, as its use cases continue to develop. Large language models (LLMs) and GenAI continue to advance at a fast pace, with the first returns on investment starting to coalesce, notably in digital advertising, where companies have demonstrated an ability to target their ads more effectively. Exciting use cases are also being proven elsewhere, including in e-commerce, finance and health care. We’re also seeing progress in manufacturing, where predictive maintenance using “digital twins” (virtual replicas of physical assets or systems that simulate real-world performance) is gaining traction. Additionally, AI developers are focused on achieving agentic AI, which we would argue is already present in at least one instance in the language capability of the models. Other areas where agentic AI is quickly taking shape include software development and deep research.
  • We are tracking other bright spots for innovation in the economy beyond AI, including space, defense, cryptocurrency, robotics, genomics and green energy. Space is undergoing a renaissance, with private-public partnerships beginning to dominate. Some longer-term goals include increased satellite efficacy and interplanetary travel. Regarding the aerospace and defense industry, a new class of companies, including Anduril and Palantir Technologies (neither is held by the strategy), are challenging the primacy of large, established defense contractors. These nimble companies are developing innovative new technologies, including autonomous drones and sophisticated military software. The cryptocurrency space is maturing, with relevant applications in smart contracts and payment systems coming to the fore. The use cases for robotics are also accelerating as AI improves the software behind the hardware. Genomics is still early in its development, with scientists and managers addressing the complexities of the medicine to achieve regulatory approval. Finally, green energy initiatives continue to advance worldwide, with the cost curve—particularly for solar energy— coming down and with new ideas around nuclear and geothermal energy grabbing attention.

data.title: Military Conflicts Mostly Haven't Hindered Long-Term Stock Growth
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

History is filled with wars, invasions, and other types of military conflict. In these times of heightened risk, investors may be tempted to reexamine their portfolio. But a long-term perspective is key to a sound investment plan. The stock market has grown for well over a century in the face of historic events big and small. Upheaval, despite how it might feel in the moment, hasn't changed that upward trajectory.

Stock market returns following geopolitical conflicts

If there’s a factor that impacts market performance, there’s an index to measure it. Geopolitical risk is no exception. The chart below illustrates 11 points in history where we experienced a peak in the Geopolitical Risk Index, and it shows the return of the S&P 500 Index 12 months after that peak. In most cases, the stock market rose significantly in the year following peak geopolitical risk.

Stocks have typically grown significantly in the 12 months following peak geopolitical risk

S&P 500 Index returns 12 months after a peak in the Geopolitical Risk Index

What long-term investors should focus on

While military conflicts understandably generate concerns about potential market impact, I believe long-term investors should focus on three questions:

1. What’s the economy’s outlook?

Ultimately, I believe investors should stay focused on the businesses that will harness innovations such as artificial intelligence and robotics, develop treatments for debilitating diseases, evolve the nation’s energy sources, and invent new technologies and industries that aren’t even on the radar. History suggests that innovations — and investment opportunities — will continue regardless of geopolitical difficulties.

2. What will the Federal Reserve do with monetary policy?

For all the focus on geopolitics, monetary policy probably matters more. The old adage holds true: Don’t fight the Fed. Historically, the economy has been hurt or helped by monetary policy conditions. It’s important that the Fed recently cut interest rates by 75 basis points in 2025 and seems poised to cut again this year.

3. Does this military conflict change the answer to either of these questions?

Typically, the answer is no, so long as the conflict remains contained or regional. And that can run counter to what some investors might expect. Consider a couple of examples. The MSCI Poland Index has been one of the world’s best-performing indexes since Russia invaded Ukraine, climbing 135.5% (24.9% per year) from the February 24, 2022, invasion through December 31, 2025.1 The MSCI Israel Index is up 102.2% (36.9% per year) since the October 7, 2023, Hamas attack.2 These aren’t outcomes many investors might have expected in the earlier days of those conflicts.

Stick to long-term investing plans

While unnerving, geopolitical conflicts shouldn’t change investors’ long-term investment plans, in my view. History has shown that other factors — economic growth, business innovation, and monetary policy — drive the path of the markets.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of January 8, 2026. Results measured in US dollars. Indexes cannot be purchased directly by investors. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. The MSCI Poland Index is designed to measure the performance of the large- and mid-cap segments of the Polish market.

  2. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of January 8, 2026. Results measured in US dollars. Indexes cannot be purchased directly by investors. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. The MSCI Israel Index is designed to measure the performance of the large- and mid-cap segments of the Israeli equity market.

Important information

NA5109676

Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions. This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures change in consumer prices as determined by the US Bureau of Labor Statistics. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices while headline CPI includes them.

The S&P 500® Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index of the 500 largest domestic US stocks. The S&P 500 Total Return Index assumes that all cash distributions are reinvested.

The MSCI Poland Index is a free-float weighted equity index. It was developed with a base value of 100 as of December 31 1992.

The MSCI Israel Index is a free-float weighted equity index. It was developed with a base value of 100 as of December 31 1992.

A basis point is one hundredth of a percentage point.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of January 8, 2026. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Military conflicts mostly haven’t hindered long-term stock growth by Invesco US

data.title: Athene Holding Preferreds Update: Still Like D The Best
data.description:
Introduction

In my last article, I gave all four Athene Insurance preferreds stocks a Buy rating with a preference for one over the others. Here I review the issuer and the issues again to see if any rating changes would be recommended. The available preferreds are:

  • Athene Holding Ltd. 6.35% Dep Sh Fix/Float Non-Cumul Pref Shares Ser A (ATH.PR.A).
  • Athene Holding Ltd. 5.625% Dep Sh Fixed Rate Non-Cumul Pref Shares Ser B (ATH.PR.B).
  • Athene Holding Ltd. 4.875% Dep Sh Rate Non-Cumul Pref Shares Ser D (ATH.PR.D).
  • Athene Holding Ltd. 7.75% Dep Sh Rate Non-Cumul Pref Shares Ser E (ATH.PR.E).

There was a fifth that was recently Called, which I always take as a sign of strength in regard to the issuer. As I did in my prior article, after weighing all important factors, D retains my top Buy rating, followed by B, A, and then E.

Athene Insurance Review

Athene is a wholly owned subsidiary of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO), but they report financials separately. That said, it would not reflect well on Apollo if they let Athene fail, which adds additional strength to what I report here.

The company-provided description is as follows:

Athene is a leading retirement services company with $430 billion of total assets as of September 30, 2025, and operations in the United States, Bermuda, Canada, and Japan. Athene is focused on providing financial security to individuals by offering an attractive suite of retirement income and savings products and also serves as a solutions provider to corporations.

Athene maintains a separate capital structure and credit profile following its merger with Apollo in 2022. It is an issuer of senior and subordinated debt, preferred stock, and also sponsors a funding agreement backed notes program, which issues senior secured medium-term notes in the bond markets.

Source: Athene.

Athene has shown constant growth in its asset base, as recently reported, and they provide a look at their long-term funding.

Since its founding in 2009, many rating agencies have raised Athene's rating two notches.

Looking at the statements provided, I calculated the following important ratios that preferred stock owners care about.

  • Dividend coverage: Income available/PFD dividends is 26X, the highest I have come across.
  • Preferred stock coverage: Using Regulatory capital, the ratio is 14X, again very strong.

I would rate the odds of either a dividend non-payment or defaulting on the preferreds as non-existent. I believe my view is supported by the A+ ratings and the fact that Athene has Apollo backing it up.

Understanding the Preferred Stocks

I compare the major features of each issue in the next table.

Factor PFD A PFD B PFD D PFD E
Issued 6/5/19 9/16/19 12/14/20 12/5/22
Size 30MM 12MM 22MM 20MM
Coupon 6.35% 5.625% 4.875% 7.75%
Call date 6/30/29 9/30/24 12/30/25 12/30/27**
Price $24.85 $20.10 $17.15 $25.73
Yield 6.4% 7.0% 7.1% 7.5%
YTC 6.7% NA NA 6.5%
Float formula 3moSOFR+.26161%+4.253% fixed fixed 5yrUST+3.962%*

* May redeem during a period from and including December 30 of each year in which there is a Reset Date to and including such Reset Date. Reset Date means December 30, 2027, and each date falls on the fifth anniversary of the preceding Reset Date.

The issues are rated BBB, with non-cumulative dividends that are eligible for the preferential tax rate.

Analysis

  • A provides the longest Call protection of the two issues where that exists.
  • A appears overpriced when comparing yields, maybe a sacrifice for the longer Call protection.
  • My belief is that once E then A float, E's formula will result in a higher coupon most periods.
  • The current forecast has E's coupon close to 8% when it starts to float, which would increase the odds of it being the first issue Called. If true, its YTC should be calculated prior to buying.
  • While current yield favors E over D, using the lower of Yield/YTC for all four issues places D first.
  • Based on coupons (fixed, then floating), Call order should be E, A, B, and D.

As I did in my prior article, weighing all those factors still results in D earning the best Buy rating, while the others are Buys in this order: B, A, then E.

Portfolio Strategy

While Athene is another financial industry issuer of preferred stocks, it is somewhat unique in that it has what I assume is the financial strength of a large owner behind it. That said, its preferreds' attributes must still be evaluated in light of what other insurance companies offer, two of which (MetLife PFDs, Prudential Notes) I recently reviewed and can be accessed by clicking on their names. Those issues yield between 5.9% and 6.3%. Being Notes, the Prudential issue's interest payments are harder to skip.

For those who want a manager to do the selecting, the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF) or VanEck Preferred Securities ex Financials ETF (PFXF) are worth a look, with PFXF yielding over 8%. My fund exposure is with the Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (RNP), which is the best performer of the three over the last decade. All three have recent articles by other Seeking Alpha contributors.

Final note

Athene Insurance also has a Note that matures in 2064, not Callable until 2029, which was recently reviewed by another Seeking Alpha contributor (article link).

data.title: JPY Slumps
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) is mostly confined to narrow ranges against most of the G10 currencies today, ahead of the US CPI report and US corporate earnings. The yen is a notable exception. The dollar reached through JPY159.00 and has barely backed off. The ostensible catalyst is the heightened speculation of a snap election, which could be announced by the end of next week. Japanese government bond yields jumped at the prospect. Comments by the finance minister that the US shares its concern about the one-way movement failed to have much impact. The market awaits clarification of President Trump’s threat to impose a 25% tariff on Iran’s trading partners, but the authority is questionable, and the implementation is not clear, though it was declared to have immediate effect. Tomorrow is another decision day for the Supreme Court, and it is possible that the ruling on the president’s use of emergency tariffs will be announced.

The US has suggested that cutting off Venezuelan oil to Cuba will starve the regime, but last year Mexico surpassed Caracas as Cuba’s largest oil source. Similarly, likely to the dismay of Washington, the EU appears to have struck a deal with China on EV imports, which includes a minimum price (and other conditions). Separately, the EU reached a deal with Mercosur, though it still needs EU Parliament approval. Lastly, the EU is in late-stage trade talks with India.

Prices

G10

• The euro’s bounce on the back of the subpoenas for the Federal Reserve stalled yesterday near $1.17, slightly above the minimum retracement of the euro’s recovery since Christmas eve. It is in an indecisive and narrow range between about $1.1655 and $1.1675, as US CPI and future Fed leadership are awaited. Nearby resistance is seen in the $1.1715-35 area.

• After initially being sold to nearly JPY157.50 yesterday, the greenback recovered in the North American session to JPY158.20 and posted its highest settlement since January 2025. Follow-through buying today lifted the US dollar to JPY159.05. The rally came after new reports played up the likelihood of a snap election and despite comments from Finance Minister Katayama who said she had talked with US Treasury Secretary Bessent at yesterday’s G7 finance ministers meeting on coordination of critical materials and both shared “concerns about the one-way weakening of the yen”. If this was an initial step on the intervention escalation ladder, the market took little notice. Options for about $650 mln at JPY158.50 expire today, and since the high was recorded, the greenback has not been much below JPY158.80.

• In sterling’s outside day yesterday, it frayed the 200-day moving average slightly below $1.3400 and reached $1.3485, which met the 50% retracement of the decline since last week’s high slightly of almost $1.3570. It settled above last Friday’s high (~$1.3450). It is in a narrow range (~$1.3460-1.3485) today as it consolidates in the upper end of yesterday’s range. About GBP500 mln in options at $1.34 expire today.

• The greenback traded inside last Friday’s range yesterday in a consolidative session against the Canadian dollar yesterday. Recall that the US dollar recorded a five-month low the day after Christmas (~CAD1.3645) and ran up to CAD1.3920 before the weekend. It is in a tight range (~CAD1.3865-1.3890) in the lower end of yesterday’s range. Options for $900 mln at CAD1.3925 expire today. Initial support is in the CAD1.3850-60 area.

• The Australian dollar reached slightly above $0.6765 last week before it retreated a cent into the weekend. Yesterday’s gain recovered a fraction more than half of its losses. The next hurdle is seen near $0.6730, but it stalled slightly above $0.6715 today and is fraying $0.6700 in the European morning. There are nearly A$2 bln of options between $0.6700 and $0.6710 that are expiring today.

EM

• More often than not at the start of the year, the dollar has traded above MXN18.00 but has failed to settle above it. The greenback has forged a shelf around MXN17.87, and a break of it signals the next leg down. The dollar is holding above MXN17.90 so far today and has spent little time above yesterday’s settlement slightly below MXN17.93.

• The dollar is recording an inside day against the offshore yuan, trading between roughly CNH7.9670 and CNH7.9760. Yesterday’s low near CNH6.9630 was the lowest the dollar has been/the strongest the yuan has been since May 2023. The PBOC shaved the dollar’s reference rate today to CNY7.0103 (from CNY7.0108 yesterday).

• The dollar made a marginal new high since the middle of last month today against the Indian rupee near INR90.30 but is now trading near session lows below INR90.19. The central bank injected $10 of liquidity through an FX swap auction.

• The US is close to agreeing to reducing the tariff on Taiwan to 15% from 20%. It would include an agreement by TSMC (TSM) to build five more chip factories in the Arizona (estimated cost ~$165 bln). It has already built one, another one is to be completed in 2028, and it has promised four more under the earlier agreement. Meanwhile, the Taiwanese dollar, which rose by a little more than 4.3% against the dollar last year (and slipped slightly against the Chinese yuan), has begun the new year off on a soft tone, falling by about 0.7% so far in January. The IMF forecast last year’s current account surplus at nearly 14% of GDP.

Other Markets

• Amid speculation of an early election, Japanese equity markets re-opened after the long holiday weekend with a bang. New record highs were recorded as the Nikkei surged 3.10% and Topix jumped 2.4%. Among the large bourses in the region, only China and India did not advance today. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is giving back the 0.20% it gained yesterday, and US index futures (SPX) are trading softer.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are rising today. In Japan, the 10-year yield popped 7.5 bp to nearly 2.16%, a new high. European benchmark yields are 2-3 bp higher. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is a couple of basis points higher to knock on the 4.20% area.

• Gold is consolidating after setting a record high yesterday near $4630. It is straddling $4600 today. Silver is firm but holding slightly below yesterday’s record high (~$86.25).

• March WTI is advancing for the fourth consecutive session. It is above $60 a barrel for the first time since mid-November. Thus far, it is holding below the 200-day moving average (~$60.60), which it has not traded above since late last September.

Data

• While the markets’ mood has been soured by the newest attack on the Fed’s independence, attention turns to the December CPI. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 0.3% increase in both the headline and core rate, which would put both at 2.7%. Fed Chair Powell has suggested that the tariffs may have boosted inflation by around 0.6%. The futures market is pricing in little chance of a hike in Q1, and the CPI is unlikely to change that assessment. St. Louis Fed President Musalem and Richmond Fed President Barkin speak today, but their views are known. October new home sales are also on tap and are seen falling by around 10.5%, but the data is too old to have much impact. The president’s order for the agencies to buy $200 bln mortgages in an attempt to lower rates may have minimal impact, according to various bank economists’ projections. The US federal government budget balance may draw some attention. The CBO estimates the deficit at $143 bln, while the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is $152.5 bln. The deficit in the calendar year through November was $1.522 trillion compared with $1.931 trillion in the first 11 months of 2024.

• Canada reports November building permits. After a nearly 15% surge in October, a modest pullback of 5.5% is the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey. The market impact is likely minimal.

• Japan’s November current account surplus rose by about JPY840 bln to JPY3.7 trillion. There had been a strong seasonal pattern of deterioration in November, but it was the third improvement in the past four years. The improved trade surplus helped. It rose to JPY625 bln from about JPY98 bln in October. Yet, despite the undervalued yen, the Japan still recorded a trade deficit last year. Still, the deficit has fallen sharply. In the first 11 months of last year, the deficit was JPY983 bln, compared with JPY3.7 bln deficit through the first 11 months of 2024.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ARKF: Portfolio Management Could Be Better, But Thematic Benefits Remain
data.description:
The ARK Blockchain & Fintech Innovation ETF (ARKF) is revisited in this article. I last covered the ETF in February 2025, where I provided a positive opinion on the basis of differentiated alpha via growth stock exposure. Given that time has passed, I decided to re-engage with the ETF; herewith are my latest thoughts.

Performance Update

The ETF has delivered high single-digit gains since we last covered it. However, most asset classes have produced good returns since then, and the ARKF ETF lagged the S&P 500 (mostly due to timing reasons).

Historical returns show that ARKF remains a higher-variance, higher-beta bet than the market index. The vehicle outperformed the market index during the past year; I attribute its performance to 1) we've lived in a low-volatility, pro-risk environment conducive to growth stocks, and 2) the "AI trade" being in full flow (the ETF is supposed to feed off of tech rallies).

Figure 3 illustrates the rolling Value-at-Risk versus the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), with no convergence evident.

Portfolio Update

Figure 4 displays ARKF's portfolio, with the left side pointing to exposure in December 2024 and the right side to exposure in September 2025 (latest reported fact sheet). The ETF's security-specific exposure has remained mostly the same since I last covered it. Notable changes include downsizing Coinbase Global (COIN) exposure, Block (XYZ) dropping out of the top 10 for Roku (ROKU), and Circle Internet Group also joining the top 10.

Revenue growth forecasts among top holdings remain robust, which is to be expected given the hyperbolic growth experienced in some of their key verticals, including blockchain, e-commerce, and fintech.

From a valuation perspective, some of the companies are trading at what I deem modest EV/EBITDA ratios (for high-growth tech stocks). Palantir (PLTR) could be deemed an outlier, but then again, the company's revenue is forecasted to scale at 96.79% next year amid growing participation in U.S. government contracts. The reason I look at EBITDA is because tech companies' net profitability can be inconsistent, given the fact that they often hold subjectively valued IP and inefficient capital structures.

Overall, underlying growth remains robust. Cyclical valuations are evident, meaning market timing should be kept in mind.

Additional Features

Despite reporting an active share of 92%, a correlation analysis (figure 7) suggests ARKF extracts a lot of its return from the general market as well as big tech, which is likely explained by market risk-on vs. risk-off flows-although security- and asset-specific risk remains evident, returns are more correlated than the active share suggests. That being said, the underlying stocks have fundamental growth premiums, which may be realised when the market shifts toward a pro-growth environment.

Additional factors I'd like to mention relate to turnover and expenses. Regarding the latter, ARKF has an expense ratio of 0.75%, which is around 65 basis points higher than SPY ETF's expense ratio. According to Seeking Alpha's database, ARKF has a turnover ratio of around 32%, which I find difficult to understand. Surely, the idea here should be to extract risk premium from thematic exposure instead of actively trading securities.

I have continuously referred to extracting risk premiums from certain sources. As a reminder, I present figure 8, which presents a better description of the fund's investment case in financial services, and figure 9, which points to consumer services - the idea is to invest in consolidation, asset-light businesses, and experiencing significant volume.

Herewith is figure 9.

Technical Price Analysis

ARKF ETF's Relative Strength Index is slightly below 50, suggesting the ETF is neither overbought nor oversold. Its price recently crossed 10- and 50-day moving averages while remaining below 100- and 200-day moving averages. This could signal a momentum trend, especially as ARKF remains adrift from the 70 RSI level (usually considered overbought). That being said, as previously mentioned, the trajectory will likely depend on the market's outlook on risk-on versus risk-off flows.

Conclusion

I think ARKF could be managed more appropriately by improving its definition of active share and lowering market beta exposure by true independent bets. That being said, I still believe the fund can play a role in a portfolio via its thematic exposure to key growth drivers within the economy. Systematically, I remain positive about the ETF's future and think technical indicators point toward a lucrative entry point.

data.title: How ICAP Delivered A 9%+ Yield While JEPI's Payout Shrank
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

There is much to love about modern technology. I grew up in a time when if you wanted to research something, you had to go to the library. Then you had to dig through index cards, decode the Dewey Decimal System, and find a book that covered the topic you wanted to research. That is, if the library you were in had such a book at all. If the book was checked out, you might be waiting a week or two.

Today, we are all walking around with the largest library ever created in our pockets. If we have a random question, we can simply speak into our phones and get an answer. Sometimes the answer is even accurate.

The amount of information that we have at our fingertips today is incredible. You can research anything you want, from the most in-depth and complex scientific theories to what your favorite celebrity prefers to eat for breakfast.

Yet, all technology comes with drawbacks, and one of those drawbacks is that people are becoming increasingly impatient. We expect to get what we want right now. When seeking information, many value speed over accuracy. Many make decisions based on incomplete information or information that is out of context.

In the investing world, it's no different. We see the market reacting to the news of the day. Investors will hold a stock for a few months and declare that it is a "horrible investment" because it hasn't gone up yet.

Patience is a virtue that is perhaps the hardest part of being a good investor.

We are going to revisit a head-to-head comparison we made three years ago.

ICAP vs. JEPI: Revisited

The Infrastructure Capital Equity Income Fund ETF (ICAP) is a pick that we discussed a few years ago. Back in 2023, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) was all the rage. Back then, JEPI was paying a yield of over 10%, and people were constantly asking us, "Why not buy JEPI?"

Here is what we wrote about JEPI:

Note that JEPI outperformed the most in the months with the steepest sell-offs in the market. Investors look at this, and also the trailing dividends, which add up to 11%+ yield, and they get excited. High yield and protected downside!

They forget two important factors:

  • The dividends are not stable. JEPI's dividend changes every month. JEPI's strategy will produce higher dividends when volatility is high. Projecting forward the current dividend is only meaningful if you expect the market to fall as aggressively in 2023 as it did in 2022. Realistically, investors should expect a dividend in the 5-8% yield range.
  • JEPI's strategy protects downside risk but gives up the benefits of the upside. When the market rallies, JEPI will only contribute a small portion of that upside.

JEPI Dividends Declined, ICAP Dividends Grew

Sure enough, since that article, JEPI has averaged $4.52/share in dividends—good for a yield around 8%. It was not the +11% many investors thought they would get. JEPI's average dividend was about 30% lower than what JEPI paid in 2022.

JEPI's dividend is highly variable, yet many investors doing their research simply looked at the "dividend yield" calculation presented on many websites, which uses a trailing-12-month dividend calculation.

In our last article, we argued that ICAP's dividend was more sustainable because it relies on a multipronged approach to covering its dividend.

ICAP does use out-of-the-money covered calls; however, its covered call strategy is targeted primarily on holdings that management believes are richly valued anyway, and they would be happy to sell.

In addition to options premium, ICAP invests primarily in dividend-paying common stocks, and it has a portion of its portfolio invested in preferred equity—providing a fixed-income component to the portfolio.

The result is that when the profitability of options declined, ICAP was still able to provide a generous dividend. In fact, ICAP increased its recurring distribution four times since we wrote the article.

We love high yield as much as the next guy, but it is crucial to understand how the dividend is being generated to predict whether it will continue in the future.

A Slow Start

Initially, our article looked bad. We wrote about ICAP at what turned out to be a peak in its price. The market declined, and ICAP's use of leverage causes it to be inherently more volatile than JEPI.

The result is that in 2023, ICAP underperformed in terms of price. An ETF that uses leverage is going to see larger price swings than one that doesn't. We'll leave it to you to read the comments in the article throughout 2023, but suffice it to say that more than one comment suggested that ICAP being down was proof that the HDO strategy doesn't work, that it is obvious that JEPI is superior, and that it being down relative to JEPI in the first 6 months was proof.

Of course, if you are a regular reader, you know that we are frequent proponents of buying with the intention to hold for at least 3-5 years. The reason is that stocks are volatile, and performance over short periods is not indicative of long-term value.

Yes, ICAP underperformed JEPI in 2023 but then outperformed it in 2024 and 2025. Source

More importantly for our income-focused strategy, it outperformed while paying out more in dividends:

While JEPI and ICAP had similar yields in 2023, ICAP's dividend has grown from $0.18/month to $0.21/month. Plus, we expect to see ICAP raise its dividend again in 2026.

Focus on the Income

We focus on the income, and that doesn't mean we just blindly look at the yield and buy the highest yield. We look at how an investment is generating cash flow and judge whether that is sustainable or not. Admittedly, sometimes we are wrong; that's being human. We passed on JEPI because JEPI relies on its derivatives strategy to generate cash flow. It was obvious in 2023 that premiums were smaller and cash flow would be lower.

ICAP also relies on options, but options are not its sole source of cash flow.

If we look at ICAP's top holdings, we see that ICAP has a value stock tilt, with many of its top 10 holdings paying decent dividends. Source

In addition to common stocks that pay a dividend, ICAP invests about 20% of its portfolio in preferred equity.

ICAP is actively managed and uses modest leverage (typically 20-30%). This is a very similar structure to another ETF we hold from the same management team: PFFA.

The result is that ICAP will be more volatile than an investment like JEPI because it uses leverage. It also has a higher allocation to "Value" stocks, which means that it won't have significant exposure to growth stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA) relative to JEPI.

Since ICAP is actively managed, investors will gain the benefit of human intelligence making decisions on which holdings to sell covered calls on, which to buy, and which to sell. This is both ICAP's greatest strength and one of its largest risks. Humans sometimes make bad decisions.

Conclusion

At the end of the day, being patient often pays off when investing. The market is an emotional place where share prices change every single day. It can become difficult to decipher the price swings that indicate permanent losses versus price movements that simply reflect the natural volatility of a publicly traded market.

This is why we focus on the income. No, we don't just buy the highest-yielding picks. When we wrote that article three years ago, JEPI had a materially higher yield. When we say, "focus on the income," we mean diving into the numbers and understanding how the investment generates income. We then make educated projections on whether we believe the dividends will be higher, lower, or about the same going forward.

You can't find that information by looking at a screener. You can't determine if an investment is "good" or "bad" long-term by looking at what the price did over a year.

Understand what the investments you buy do, how they generate income, and then make a decision to invest for the long term. Investors who have patience will be rewarded.

data.title: How The 5% Rule Quietly Changed My Dividend Growth Strategy
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past couple of weeks, I have written two retirement-focused articles. On Jan. 2, I discussed the model portfolio I would buy if I were to retire right now. On Jan. 9, I discussed the ETF version if I were to avoid buying any single stocks. Essentially, it's the same strategy but a different approach.

In both of these articles, I made clear that I have no intention of retiring anytime soon. That's solely based on the fact that I really love what I do. If someone were to wire me $10 million today, I wouldn't change a thing.

Well, I may buy a few fancy toys, but job-wise, I wouldn't change a thing.

Also, for those of you who are new to my articles, I'm 30, which means I'm decades away from the legal retirement age and way too young to even consider "not working," whatever that may look like.

Nonetheless, there are two reasons why covering retirement and higher-yielding investments is a big part of my job.

  1. A large part of my reader base consists of retirees. For these people, it would add zero value if I were to discuss (dividend) growth stocks all day.
  2. I'm working on a retirement plan, as I'm entirely dependent on my own finances. Whether it's my future retirement or financial security in a job-loss situation, I'm the one in charge of my financial future.

Because of point 2, I automatically cover point 1, as everyone on this website has a front-row seat when it comes to my financial plans, my investments, and everything else that goes on between my ears.

This also includes the consistent balance between safety and growth.

On the one hand, safety is very important to me, as the margin of error is simply too slim for me to afford costly financial mistakes. On the other hand, too much safety could block my efforts to grow my wealth as fast as possible.

I think the chart below, which shows the total return of long-term bonds and the tech-focused QQQ ETF (QQQ), perfectly shows the safety vs. growth dilemma. One has higher returns and more volatility, while the other has low volatility yet way lower returns.

The example above is obviously a bit extreme, but I think you get my point.

That's why I came up with the 5% Rule, which I think should be named the 5% Target. It's a concept I first discussed in great detail on Aug. 15, when I called it "The Easiest Retirement Investing Strategy I'll Ever Share."

In this article, I'll elaborate on that strategy, explain how my strategy has changed, and show you why I even reworked the way I approach stock valuation a bit.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

The 5% Rule Helps Me Build Without Losing Focus

When I first brought up the 5% Rule, the most important thing was to explain what it was not instead of what it was, as weird and vague as that may sound to you right now.

This rule wasn't a call to buy the highest-yielding stocks on the market but a framework that exists because too many people chase yield, which can lead to very poor outcomes, as I have often discussed.

When we chase high income, we may end up owning companies with unsustainable dividends and poor business models that end up giving us both dividend cuts and capital losses. In other words, chasing yield can lead to extreme value destruction.

The SuperDividend ETF (SDIV) is a good proxy for that thesis. Here's what it's about:

SDIV accesses 100 of the highest dividend paying equities around the world, potentially increasing a portfolio's yield. - GlobalX

Currently, this ETF yields roughly 10%. Moreover, it sells companies that are either close to cutting their dividend (one might expect a cut) or those that have cut their dividend.

If you had invested in this ETF in June 2011, which is when it went public, you would have made 16% since then. That's its total return, meaning it includes all dividends. In other words, you would have trailed the market by a huge margin, while all dividends were needed to keep you from losing money.

My 5% Rule tries to avoid these risks by focusing on two questions:

  • How much capital will I need when I retire?
  • What level of income can I generate without taking big risks?

Here's a key quote I have used in prior articles on this subject, including on Jan. 2:

So, what really is the 5% Rule, you may ask?

For starters, if I had to summarize this strategy, it would be something like this: I'm convinced that 5% is the sweet spot between buying income and dividend growth without taking on the risks of buying a "sucker yield."

Or to put it differently, I believe I can build fantastic dividend income portfolios picking from stocks yielding close to 5%.

See, right now, the risk-free rate, using the 10-year U.S. government bond rate, is 4.2%. That's the income you can buy with minimal risk, as we can assume this debt will always be repaid. Technically speaking, of course.

The wider the spread between a dividend yield and this rate, the higher the risk. There are obviously outliers for various reasons, but generally speaking, if a stock trades at a yield that is substantially higher than the risk-free rate, we have to ask ourselves why the market is allowing us to buy a yield that high. Where's the catch?

Right now, I believe we can buy top-tier income between 5% and 6% without having to worry too much about any yield traps. That's what my aforementioned retirement articles were focused on.

With that said, when I started investing, I focused on maintaining an average yield of roughly 3%. That was based on the tax system at the time in the Netherlands, as I assumed if I just kept my investments "forever," I could pay the wealth tax from income without having to sell anything.

That made sense. On paper, at least.

However, now, the tax system will be changed. And, besides that, I'm leaving for a country that taxes capital gains instead of wealth. So, that game plan is out the window.

And, to be honest, that's a good thing, as my strategy to maintain a 3% yield kept me from buying the best companies in each sector. Although I believe that every company I ever bought for my dividend (growth) portfolio is a high-quality player, it didn't make much sense for my portfolio.

To give you a few examples, I should not have bought Lockheed Martin (LMT) but TransDigm (TDG), which is a supplier with superior pricing power, higher growth, and much more aggressive management. Now, I own TDG.

This is what the performance difference between the two looks like:

While I do not regret buying Union Pacific (UNP), as it's still one of my best investment ideas, it was my mistake not to buy a much faster-growing transportation company like Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL) as well. I own it now, but I missed a few years of compounding.

Initially, I bought utilities for income. This included Duke Energy (DUK). Duke is a well-run utility but a horrible choice for investors my age, let alone my portfolio when I was 25.

I could go on, but that's basically the gist of it.

One of the most important considerations is that income does not compound. Capital compounds. That makes dividends much more powerful if you have enough capital. Before that point, dividends are important but not crucial.

Now, don't get me wrong, I'm still a dividend growth investor. However, over the past two years, my focus has shifted to the growth component, as I don't care about maintaining a certain average yield. I want to own businesses with high dividend growth and compounding power.

As long as I have a path to the 5% Rule, I know I can eventually make a quick shift to income whenever I have to.

For example, I know that if I wanted to retire in Albania, I could live like a king off $2,000 per month. That's because I pay no rent, have no mortgage, and because most expenses are really low.

Applying my 5% rule would mean that I need close to $500,000 in assets for a post-tax yield of 5.0%. That has been my goal for a while. I'm now way past it, yet I'm not changing my 5% Rule, as I would like to have much more spending power once I retire.

How I'm Shifting Even My View on Valuations

Sometimes, people ask me if I still care about valuations. From these questions, I get that it's mostly based on some of my largest investments, which are not the kind of "obvious" deep value ideas that many others cover here on Seeking Alpha.

For example, I don't really focus on a stock that trades at 10x earnings, while its long-term average is 17x earnings. Although that's part of my research, none of my biggest investments were based on that methodology.

I focus a bit more on "hidden" value.

  • Old Dominion Freight Line was "never" officially cheap, as this trucking company still traded close to 30x earnings. This was due to the freight recession. My case was that if I'm right about a cyclical recovery, EPS estimates would come up, making ODFL look cheaper. Right now, that looks to be the case, as the market has started to price in a cyclical recovery (see below). I'm now up 14% on a position that I have consistently bought on weakness since 2024.

  • Both Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB) are special cases among energy-focused stocks, as they are ultra-high-margin landowners. For decades, people have made the case that it's absurd to charge a premium for this business model.
  • My second-biggest investment, QXO (QXO), which overtook TPL, is a thesis entirely focused on the long-term M&A plans of Brad Jacobs. As I have often said, "nobody" who buys QXO cares about the next quarter, as they know this company will look very different a few years from now.

See, in all cases, I care a lot about valuations, just in a slightly different way.

The other day, I read a fascinating article on valuations that perfectly describes how I focus on the growth component of my 5% Rule (the road to retirement, so to speak). The article made the case that we shouldn't focus on P/E ratios to assess how attractive a company is. Or, at least, it should be just a part of the assessment.

The most important things are:

  • A high and durable ROIC (return on invested capital).
  • A long runway for investments and reinvestments.
  • A healthy balance sheet.
  • Well-balanced payouts (this is about dividends).

ROIC is the basis of it all, as that number shows how effective a company is when it comes to investing capital. Basically, it's post-tax EBIT (earnings before interest and taxes) divided by the total value of debt + equity - excess cash.

If a company is good at generating high returns, that's obviously a benefit. However, there's more to it, as a healthy balance sheet is also important. If my ROIC is 20% but my cost of capital is 21%, I'm destroying value with every transaction. So, a low cost of capital is key, too.

And, on top of that, I need a lot of investment opportunities. For example, if I'm really good at investing in real estate, yet I cannot find a deal, I cannot generate value, as there's no place where I can put my money.

I hope all of this makes sense so far.

For example, Altria (MO) is one of the market's favorite income stocks. I think it's fair to say that, given how much coverage it gets and how many people own it. Altria has an ROIC of roughly 40%, which is absolutely exceptional. However, based on what we just discussed, the return from a good investment is based on this:

Return = ROIC x reinvestment rate (simplified long-term estimate!!!)

Altria has a very high ROIC, yet because smoking is in a long-term secular decline and we can assume that it takes a long time for alternatives to become dominant (I'm painting with a super broad brush here), the company does not have a lot of places to put its money.

That's why it has a dividend yield of roughly 7.5% with a payout ratio of 80%.

This explains why the Altria total return has been a bit subdued, as the majority of its earnings go to shareholders instead of places where it can benefit from its high ROIC.

A company like Carlisle Companies (CSL), which has become a pure-play housing supplier in recent years, has an ROIC of nearly 30%, as we can see below. This company also has a healthy balance sheet and a lot of room to grow, as it consistently buys smaller peers, invests in new products, and reinvests a large part of its capital, as its 1.2% dividend has a payout ratio of a mere 22%, which means 78% "stays in the company."

Despite the recent housing slump, the stock has done tremendously well, as we can see below.

MO and CSL are just two examples.

However, the article I just mentioned had a lot of findings from extensive research.

Here's what they found:

  • If a company with an ROIC of 18% reinvests 100% of its capital, it returns 17% per year over 30 years.
  • Even if the exit multiple were to be cut in half compared to the entry multiple, the return would be 14%. This means that if the P/E ratio at the point of investing in this company were twice as high as the multiple we get when selling, the return would still be 14% per year.
  • A company with an ROIC of 6% that returns all of its earnings to shareholders returns just 5% to 6% per year, even if the exit multiple is higher than the entry multiple.

Again, these are all theoretical findings. All stocks are individual. However, it shows how important the combo of ROICs, reinvestment rates, and dividends is.

That's why the article made the case that the combination of a high ROIC and elevated reinvestment rate is more important than the combo of margins and EPS growth. As long as a company is good at putting capital to work and has room to do so, it can achieve superior returns.

Now, I have to add that valuations still matter. If we buy into a seriously inflated P/E ratio, we may see subdued returns, as it puts tremendous pressure on the ROIC and reinvestment rate to "get the job done." If we get a high-quality company at a good price, we can add the valuation as a tailwind.

That's why I have been buying so much CSL and ODFL (among others) last year.

Unfortunately, finding these gems isn't easy. Schroders, which researched all of this, noted that less than 1% of companies in their sample met their highest criteria. That makes sense, as Bessembinder (2017) found that just 4% of all U.S. companies accounted for the majority of all stock market gains.

This makes finding gems hard but also very rewarding.

So, to go back to my main theme, unless you're less than a decade away from retirement, I believe obsessing over yield is very counterproductive. This includes putting too much faith into "traditional" valuation metrics like P/E ratios.

Quality matters more, and if we follow the 5% Rule, we can plan ahead, figure out how much capital we need, and then grow our way to that target.

That means the key is understanding where you are on your journey to retirement, figuring out how much you need, and then working on a low-risk game plan to eventually squeeze more income out of your hard-earned portfolio.

Ever since I realized this, my net worth has risen much faster than I expected and put me even ahead of my most bullish retirement plans. That's no guarantee, but given that this thesis is based on a lot of research, I think the theoretical framework is in our favor.

Takeaway

I'm still a dividend investor. I cannot mention that enough.

However, today, we spent time discussing an important strategic shift of mine and how I have used the 5% Rule to better align my targets with my investment approach. Now, I'm building toward a situation where a 5-6% yield pays for all potential expenses, while I can fully focus on getting there using higher-growth stocks, often selected based on their ability to put capital to work instead of their P/E ratios alone (painting with a broad brush).

The dividend is key, but it's not everything. Consistently rising dividends are a sign of strength, but if it makes it harder for a company to exploit investment opportunities, the total return may suffer in the long term.

When I realized that, my personal returns improved as well.

The 5% Rule didn't change, but I finally learned how to apply it correctly.

So, unless you're retired and sustainable income trumps everything, we should prioritize quality over income. Safe income will likely follow automatically over time.

And, even if you're retired and looking to add some growth to your portfolio, focusing on ROICs and similar metrics may help you juice the growth profile of your portfolio a bit.

Needless to say, going forward, I'll provide a lot more actionable ideas and focus on both higher-income opportunities and companies that excel at capital allocation.

data.title: Iran Claims Control, But How Will The U.S. Respond?
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here is the latest in trending:

Messy merger: Paramount (PSKY) files a lawsuit against Warner Bros. (WBD), which just had a big night at the Golden Globes. Here are the odds of Netflix (NFLX) closing the deal.

Earnings season: Delta (DAL) and JPMorgan (JPM) are set to kick off Q4 festivities this morning. Explore the top- and lowest-rated large-cap stocks ahead of the reporting cycle.

Market reversal: Wall Street ends at records, shrugging off concerns over the recent Powell investigation. Check out the Trump-Powell timeline, but does the DOJ probe mark the end of Fed independence?

Bloody crackdown

Security forces have "full control" of the Islamic Republic, according to Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, following two weeks of protests and unrest. Reports suggest otherwise, but it is hard to discern information with an internet blackout and other communications largely cut off from the outside world. Various death toll figures of civilians have also been cited, ranging from several hundred to at least 12,000.

What's next: President Trump is still weighing a response that can include military strikes or cyberattacks, but in the meantime, he has opened the channel for diplomacy while hitting the country economically. "Effective immediately, any Country doing business with the Islamic Republic of Iran will pay a Tariff of 25% on any and all business being done with the United States of America," he wrote on Truth Social. "This Order is final and conclusive. Thank you for your attention to this matter!" See Iran's top trading partners

Oil is another big risk if Supreme Leader Khamenei decides to disrupt traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, and may also explain recent U.S. moves in Venezuela that can help shore up supply. About a quarter of the world’s seaborne oil passes through the critical chokepoint, as well as significant LNG exports. Any action brings its own risks, given all of the parties involved, and whether that will ultimately be enough to address Iran's currency crisis and economic mismanagement that triggered the protests in the first place.

Thought bubble: While some kind of military action is likely, President Trump doesn't necessarily want regime change, but rather a regime that will be submissive to America's global priorities and leadership. In fact, it's a playbook that's already in place even for allies, with Trump preferring administrations where he can make demands from a "stronger" place of power, rather than coming to the table from a "weaker" place of shared values. It also helps understand why he wants current governments, like the ones in Venezuela, Cuba, and Syria, to remain intact. That can all change in Iran if the population successfully fights for new leadership, but unless there is broad and visible support, don't expect Trump to go all in on "failed" regime change. (1 comment)

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

How A $600,000 Portfolio Can Produce $5,000 In Monthly Dividends

Energy Transfer: Game Changing FY26 Guidance

Novo Nordisk: My Top Pick For 2026

AI Reset Is Complete; Tech's Next Leg Starts Here

2 Mega Dividends Designed To Reward Loyalty; Yields +7.5%

What else is happening...

WSB survey results: Credit card rewards over interest rate caps.

Google (GOOGL) becomes fourth company to hit $4T milestone.

OpenAI (OPENAI) to air Super Bowl ad for second year in a row.

Trump to tour Ford (F) factory, promote manufacturing in Detroit.

GM (GM) CEO still sees EVs as 'endgame' despite industry reset.

Ackman says prepayment penalties could lower mortgage rates.

Meta (META) to cut virtual reality staff to allocate more for AI.

User safety: More legal action against Grok's deepfake images.

Exxon (XOM) remains 'interested' in sending team to Venezuela.

Latest steps to diversify rare earth supply chains away from China.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +3.1%. Hong Kong +0.9%. China -0.6%. India -0.3%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.1%. Paris -0.6%. Frankfurt -0.1%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.1%. S&P -0.1%. Nasdaq -0.3%. Crude +1.7% to $60.49. Gold -0.5% to $4,594.10. Bitcoin +1.6% to $92,011.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +2 bps to 4.20%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Bank of New York Mellon (BK).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Ciena: Connectivity AI/Datacenter Boom In Full Swing
data.description:
Shares of Ciena (CIEN) have been one of these stocks that seemed to stagnate and have gotten a lift around the craze of AI. Ciena, as one of the poster child stocks around the dot-com bubble, has seen large setbacks and struggles ever since.

Hard to believe, but a current $230 stock was just a $50 stock during the tariff talks in the spring of last year, with shares five-folding in less than a year, as the AI craze is now lifting many boats. Accelerating current momentum, including the creation of AI and datacenter strategies and performance, and a convincing 2026 outlook sent shares higher.

However, shares have seen incredible momentum, so much that one can really ask if too much momentum has been seen here, as I err towards a positive reaction on the question.

All About Connectivity

Ciena describes its business as being a leader in global high-speed connectivity, building the most adaptive networks that allow customers to meet ever-increasing digital demands. Founded back in 1992 in Maryland, the company and its stock really participated in the momentum run at the time of the dot-com crisis, and after a tougher period of time, they have come to life again.

The company generates some $4 billion in sales (in fiscal 2024) from providing such services to its clients. The company employs over 8,000 workers, nearly half of whom are R&D specialists, with the business serving over 1,600 customers across the globe.

The rise of AI drives unparalleled growth in bandwidth consumption, driving demand for Ciena's software control, automation, programmable infrastructure, analytics, and intelligence services. Its solutions span wide-area networks like subsea, long-haul regional, and metro services, as well as in and around datacenters.

Looking at the performance of the business, similar trends have been seen. A $2.6 billion business in 2016 saw revenue rise to $4.0 billion in 2024, but accounting for inflation and bolt-on M&A efforts, that translates into more modest growth, but still pretty reasonable. GAAP operating margins have been reported in the mid- to high-single digits, which is not too convincing, all amidst a pretty flattish share count.

About the Base Valuation

In December of 2024, the company reported its fiscal 2024 results, that is, for the year ending in November of that year. Reported revenues that year were even down 8% to $4.01 billion, although fourth-quarter sales were flat at $1.12 billion. Adjusted operating margins for the year took a 340-basis point hit to 9.7% of sales, and with EBITDA down 28% to $481 million, no signs of an imminent AI boom were seen.

Adjusted earnings for the year were down $0.90 to $1.82 per share, as GAAP earnings only came in at $0.58 per share. With much of the reconciliation due to stock-based compensation expenses, I saw realistic earnings closer to GAAP earnings than the adjusted earnings. All this made it difficult to get too upbeat on the shares.

On Fire

The company started the fiscal year 2025 with a 3% increase in first-quarter sales to $1.07 billion. Second quarter sales rose by as much as 23% to $1.13 billion, although, truth be told, a big part of the growth acceleration came from the soft performance this time in 2024, making for easy comparables. This momentum is driven by strong growth in the core optical networking business.

In September, Ciena reported a 29% increase in third-quarter revenues to $1.22 billion, with the top-line sales momentum now driven by margin gains as well. All this is driven by continued upbeat news on the corporate front, including orders, new technologies, etc.

In December, Ciena reported a 20% increase in third-quarter sales to $1.35 billion, as full-year revenues are up 19% to $4.77 billion. Full-year adjusted earnings rose by $0.82 to $2.64 per share after fourth-quarter earnings came in as high as $0.91 per share. GAAP earnings are up less pronounced to $0.85 per share for the year, as the adjustment relating to stock-based compensation expenses topped a dollar per share on a pre-tax basis. This makes realistic earnings come in somewhere in between, which I estimate to be between $1.50 and $2.00 per share.

However, the company is seeing strong momentum, as is evident in the fourth quarter results, but also the outlook for 2026. The company guided for 2026 sales at a midpoint of $5.9 billion, plus or minus $200 million. Non-GAAP margins for the year are seen around 17% of sales. Such margin gains are huge, after such margins only came in at 11.2% in 2025.

This suggests that adjusted operating margins around $530 million might advance to $1 billion, essentially doubling, with adjusted earnings seen doubling to well over $5 per share and realistic earnings likely somewhere near $3-4 per share. Note that a tiny part of this momentum is driven by the purchase of Nubis Communications, as announced in September. This $270 million cash deal adds further technologies to the business, including key strengths in co-packaged optics/near-packaged optics, as well as electrical ACC.

Current Expectations

With 145 million shares of Ciena now trading at $230, shares have even come down from a peak around $260 per share, and the market value of Ciena has risen to $33 billion and change here. This elucidates a tiny net debt load, which is not relevant in the grand scheme of the valuation discussion, although this will tick up a bit following the purchase of Nubis.

Right now, valuations have risen to about 5-6 times sales, but these are rapidly becoming higher-margin sales, with earnings seen making giant advancements this year. However, a realistic earnings number around $3-4 per share translates into a very demanding 57-77 times earnings multiple here.

All this has driven a huge re-rating in the shares, this just being a $7 billion business at $50 per share, as some $25 billion in expectations have been built into the shares since spring of last year. While some of this is clearly understandable given the accelerating growth and margin gains seen already, the issue is that expectations have risen much further than certainly the results and also the convincing 2026 outlook.

All this comes as the company has said it is rapidly becoming a datacenter play, with the company announcing investor events around its innovation program, as well as its AI and datacenter strategy. While this is to be applauded, the current share price factors in a lot of (sustainable) growth.

Having seen such situations before, there are fundamental reasons to be upbeat, yet at the same time, I am urging caution as well. The degree of valuation multiple inflation, uneven past performance, and concerns about the boom element of it all make the position very risky after such a degree of shareholder price momentum has already been seen.

While Ciena's role in the AI/datacenter boom is unmistakably there, a boom in business performance does not automatically create great shareholder price returns, certainly not at this point in the cycle.

data.title: 2025 Ends On Down Note For Younger Teens Employment Situation
data.description:
The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics released its employment situation data through December 2025, resuming their regular reporting schedule after the delays caused by 2025's Senate Democrats' government shutdown fiasco.

The jobs data was mixed for working U.S. teens. Older teens, age 18-19, saw their numbers among the ranks of the employed rise in December 2025. With a seasonally adjusted 3,547,000 older teens earning wages, the percentage of the older teen population rebounded to 42.9%.

Unfortunately, younger teens, age 16-17, saw their numbers fall again, dropping to a seasonally adjusted 1,938,000 and continuing a multi-year, long-running trend. That downward trend is most evident when looking at the share of the younger teen population with jobs. The employment-to-population statistic for this demographic dropped to 20.3%, its lowest level since January 2021's 20.4%.

The following chart visualizes these trends along with the seasonally adjusted total age 16-19 employment level:

Sharp-eyed readers will catch that the number of employed age 16-17 teens and age 18-19 teens doesn't add up to the combined age 16-19 figures in these charts. That's because each age demographic gets its own seasonal adjustment. If you want numbers that do add up within a small margin of error, you'll want to access the BLS' non-seasonally adjusted employment figures.

Really sharp-eyed readers will also note the seasonally adjusted data presented in the charts doesn't match our last edition in this series. The BLS revised its seasonally adjusted data with the December 2025 employment situation report, with the revisions affecting all previously reported data from January 2021 onward. The revisions do not affect the nonseasonally adjusted data, which remains unchanged from their previously reported figures.

Reference

U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Labor Force Statistics (Current Population Survey - CPS). [Online Database]. Accessed: 9 January 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
